title: Sales
summary: Sales as a traction channel is direct, human-driven customer acquisition through conversations — generating leads, qualifying them, and converting them into paying customers. This chapter teaches the pipeline math that determines whether the channel is viable, the structural conditions (ACV, hand-holding, complexity) under which it fits, the tactics that actually work (SPIN, PNAME, A/B/C bucketing), the discipline of founder-led sales before hiring, and how to test the channel cheaply before committing a full sales team.

concepts.title: What Sales Is as a Traction Channel

pretest.prompt: A founder says, 'Sales is our channel — we're going to hire two AEs next month and start calling people.' Before reading on: what's the difference between 'sales' as the traction channel described in this book and 'sales' as a general business function? What specific property of a product makes sales-the-channel a sensible acquisition strategy rather than a default activity?
pretest.modelAnswer: Every company eventually has a sales function — someone signs contracts. But sales as one of the nineteen traction channels is narrower: it's the channel where new customers are acquired primarily through direct, human-driven conversations (calls, demos, meetings) rather than through self-serve funnels, ads, content, or viral mechanics. It fits when the product requires interpersonal hand-holding before a purchase — typically because the deal is expensive, the buying process is complex, or the prospect needs to be taught why the problem even matters. Low-ACV, self-serve consumer products don't fit: the unit economics don't support the cost of a human per deal. So the test isn't 'do we have people who can talk' but 'does our product's price and complexity make a human conversation a load-bearing step in acquisition?' Hiring AEs before answering that question is putting a team in front of a channel that may not exist.

lesson.markdown:
## The definition

Sales, as a traction channel, is **the process of generating leads, qualifying them, and converting them into paying customers through direct human-driven conversations** — calls, emails, meetings, demos. It's not the everything-a-company-eventually-needs 'sales function.' It's a specific acquisition mechanism that sits alongside SEO, viral, paid, content, and the other sixteen channels as one of the ways customers arrive.

The load-bearing property: **the product requires interpersonal interaction before a purchase**. That's usually because the deal is expensive, the buying process is complex, the prospect needs to be educated about the problem, or the purchase spans multiple stakeholders with different concerns. When any of those are true, a self-serve funnel leaks most of its prospects at the moment they hit a question no webpage can answer. A human on the other end of a call can answer, reassure, customize, and negotiate — and close the deal the funnel would have lost.

## Why the channel framing matters

Founders talk about sales in two very different ways, and conflating them produces a lot of bad decisions.

One meaning is operational: every B2B company eventually has people whose job is to sign contracts. This is sales-as-a-function, and it exists whether or not sales is your traction channel.

The other meaning — the one this chapter cares about — is sales-as-a-traction-channel: the primary mechanism by which your next 100, 1,000, or 10,000 customers are acquired. In that framing, sales competes with the eighteen other channels on the same question Bullseye asks of all of them: for our stage, our ICP, and our price point, is this the channel that will produce enough customers, at acceptable cost, to move us forward?

The bad decisions come from skipping the second question. Founders assume B2B = sales channel, hire a VP of Sales, and six months later discover the product is too cheap per seat to support a human-closed deal, or that prospects aren't actually buyable by calling — they're buyable through content that ranks when a procurement team searches.

## Hand-holding is the signal

The practical test: can a prospect read your landing page, click a button, and become a customer without talking to you? If yes, sales is probably not your primary channel — you have a self-serve product and should focus on the channels that feed self-serve funnels. If no — if the prospect predictably has questions that require a conversation, references to check, integrations to validate, budget to justify, legal to involve — then sales is doing real work pedagogically, not just closing what marketing already sold.

That hand-holding is where the channel earns its cost. A salesperson isn't a price list with legs. They exist because the prospect's questions are legitimate and can't be answered efficiently any other way.

## What this chapter is about

Scaling sales as a channel requires designing and implementing a **repeatable sales model** — a system that reliably turns leads into paying customers at a predictable rate. The rest of the chapter unpacks that system: the first-customer conversations that make it possible, the pipeline math that tells you whether it works, the tactics that produce qualified leads and closed deals, and the pitfalls that sink founders who treat sales as a black box they can hire away.

workedExample.setup: Two founders each say 'sales is our traction channel.' Rafa sells ContractIQ, a $48K/year legal contract-review tool for mid-market general counsel — ICP is 2,000 companies globally, average deal takes 4 months, procurement requires a demo and a reference call. Aisha sells NotePop, a $12/month consumer note-taking app with a free tier — signup is email-only, users are individuals, there's no buying committee.
workedExample.walkthrough:
Apply the definition. Sales-as-a-channel requires interpersonal interaction to be *load-bearing* in the purchase.

**Rafa (ContractIQ).** Walk the prospect's path: a general counsel hits the site, watches a demo video, but then has real questions — how does this integrate with our document management system? Is it SOC 2 compliant? Can legal redact sensitive clauses before your model trains on them? Who else has deployed this? None of those are answerable by self-serve. Procurement wants a reference call. Legal wants a security questionnaire filled out. The deal is $48K — budget needs sign-off. At every step, a human conversation is not optional. Sales is the channel. Moreover, the unit economics support it: at $48K ACV, spending 10–20 hours of AE time per deal and giving up 20–30% to commission still leaves healthy margin.

**Aisha (NotePop).** Walk the prospect's path: someone searches 'best note app,' lands on the site, hits 'Sign up free,' uses it for a week, upgrades to $12/month with a credit card. Total touches from Aisha: zero. At $144 annual revenue per converted user, there is no budget for a human — even one hour of $80/hr sales-rep time consumes most of year-one revenue. If Aisha hired an SDR to call individual prospects, she'd destroy her unit economics to produce users who would have signed up on their own. Her channels are the ones that feed self-serve funnels: SEO, content, viral loops, paid acquisition with a low CAC target.

Rafa's 'sales is our channel' is a real claim. Aisha's is a category error — she'd be hiring a sales function for a product whose customers don't need one. Same two words, very different decisions. The chapter is about Rafa's case; Aisha should rerun Bullseye and put sales in Column C.

contrast.explanation: Sales is one of the nineteen traction channels, not a default assumption for any B2B company. Founders often treat 'we're B2B, so sales is our channel' as automatic. That's the familiarity-bias failure Chapter 1 warns about in a different costume. Sales earns its spot by surviving the Bullseye brainstorm + rank + test process, same as viral, content, SEM, or trade shows. A B2B company with low ACV and a self-serve product may be far better served by SEO or content marketing; a consumer company with a high-price subscription and complex onboarding may actually need a light-touch sales motion. The channel is defined by the fit, not the industry.

flashcards.front: Define sales as a traction channel.
flashcards.back: The channel where new customers are acquired primarily through direct, human-driven conversations — generating leads, qualifying them, and converting them into paying customers. It fits when the product requires interpersonal interaction before a purchase, typically because of high price, complexity, or a multi-stakeholder buying process.

flashcards.front: Which of these is sales working as a traction channel (versus sales as a generic business function)? (a) a $29/mo note app that hires a customer-support rep to handle billing calls. (b) a $50K/year analytics platform where AEs run 20 demos a week to convert procurement-led evaluations. (c) a viral consumer app whose founder occasionally takes meetings with press.
flashcards.back: (b). Sales-as-a-channel is the primary acquisition mechanism, requiring human-driven conversations because the purchase is expensive and complex. (a) is a support function, not a channel. (c) is PR-adjacent, not sales.

flashcards.front: Sales as a traction channel fits when the product requires ______ before a purchase — typically because the deal is expensive, the buying process is ______, or the prospect needs to be educated about the problem.
flashcards.back: interpersonal interaction ; complex — these are the structural conditions under which a human conversation does load-bearing work in acquisition.

flashcards.front: What's the quick diagnostic for whether sales is your primary channel?
flashcards.back: Can a prospect realistically move from landing page to paid customer without talking to you? If yes, sales is probably not your primary channel. If no — if their questions, integrations, references, and approvals predictably require a human — sales is doing real work in the purchase, and the channel is plausible.

flashcards.front: Why does scaling sales as a channel require a 'repeatable sales model' specifically?
flashcards.back: Because a channel has to produce customers at a predictable rate and cost. A hero founder closing bespoke deals is evidence of demand but not yet a scalable channel. A repeatable sales model — consistent lead source, qualification criteria, call structure, conversion rates — is what lets you forecast pipeline, hire against it, and compare sales to other traction channels on the same terms.

scenarios.setup: A founder building a $19/month freelancer-invoicing SaaS says: 'We're going to do sales as our main channel because B2B SaaS is always a sales-driven business.'
scenarios.question: Is sales a plausible primary channel here? Why or why not, and what would you recommend instead?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Not plausible. The reasoning 'B2B SaaS = sales channel' is familiarity bias — it treats the industry as the answer rather than the fit. At $19/month ($228/year), a single hour of AE time (loaded cost often $80–150/hr) wipes out most of the first-year revenue. You cannot have humans close deals at that price point without destroying unit economics. The product also sounds self-serve: freelancers don't convene buying committees or run procurement on $19/mo tools — they swipe a card. The real channels here are likely SEO (freelancers search 'best invoicing tool'), content marketing (how-to-invoice posts that rank), and possibly community building. Sales belongs in Column C unless the product adds an enterprise tier where a multi-seat deal can support human time.
rubric: Rejects 'B2B SaaS = sales' as familiarity bias, not analysis
Does the ACV math — human time costs vs. per-customer revenue
Notes the self-serve nature of the buyer (no procurement, no committee)
Recommends specific alternative channels matched to the product shape
Leaves the door open for sales at a higher-ACV tier

scenarios.setup: A founder selling a $30K/year HR compliance platform insists 'we'll make sales work through pure content marketing and self-serve signup — no human in the loop.' She's confident because their onboarding videos are very good.
scenarios.question: Walk through why this framing may undershoot the channel reality, and what a realistic customer-acquisition path looks like for a $30K ACV enterprise product.
scenarios.modelAnswer: At $30K ACV sold to HR leaders, the buying path almost certainly involves questions content alone can't answer: security reviews, HRIS integrations, SOC 2 documentation, legal redlines, procurement forms, multi-stakeholder approval (HR + IT + Legal + Finance). Excellent onboarding videos help but don't replace the conversations where a prospect wants a live answer to 'can you handle our specific payroll provider?' or 'will you sign our MSA instead of yours?' Treating a $30K enterprise product as fully self-serve isn't impossible, but it's the exception and usually requires an unusually opinionated, no-custom-work product. The realistic path: marketing (content, SEO, targeting blogs) feeds the top of the funnel; a sales team qualifies leads, runs demos, navigates the buyer's committee, and closes. The founder's mistake is conflating 'we can automate top-of-funnel' with 'we don't need humans in the loop at all.' The correct frame is that content + sales interlock — content gets the lead, sales closes the deal. Removing sales entirely at $30K ACV typically leaves most of the demand uncaptured.
rubric: Identifies that $30K ACV enterprise products almost always involve committee buying
Names specific conversation-required moments (security, integrations, legal, procurement)
Distinguishes 'automate lead-gen' from 'remove sales entirely'
Prescribes the correct interlock: marketing feeds top of funnel, sales closes
Doesn't dismiss self-serve entirely — notes the rare exception

scenarios.setup: A team has just hit $800K ARR for their $24K/year data-infrastructure product, 100% through founder-led sales. They're debating whether to declare 'sales is our traction channel' and hire a full sales org, or to treat the current traction as a validated demand signal and rerun Bullseye to compare sales against other channels (content, partnerships, targeting data-engineering blogs).
scenarios.question: Which move is more disciplined, and what's the principle behind your answer?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Rerunning Bullseye is the more disciplined move, even though sales has visibly produced revenue. The principle: traction on one channel proves the product can be sold, not that sales is the best channel for the next stage of growth. Founder-led sales routinely closes deals that wouldn't close through any other path, because a founder carries conviction and domain knowledge no AE will match. Extrapolating from founder-sales success to 'sales is our channel at scale' is a classic error — the conversion rates, close speeds, and CAC under founder-sales are often not reproducible by a hired team. The better move is to treat $800K as validation of demand and now run a proper brainstorm/rank/prioritize: maybe content marketing + inbound sales produces a 3x cheaper CAC; maybe partnerships with cloud providers produces a 10x larger pipeline. Sales may still win the rerun — but it has to earn it with actual comparative data, not default in because it's what worked when the founder was doing everything. Skipping this step and hiring a VP of Sales is how many companies burn 18 months discovering their real channel was somewhere else.
rubric: Recommends rerunning Bullseye, not defaulting to 'scale what worked'
Distinguishes founder-led sales performance from hired-team performance
Articulates that traction on one channel validates demand, not channel choice
Names alternative channels that should be tested comparatively
Flags the cost of a premature VP of Sales hire

teachback.prompt: Explain, in 60 seconds, what sales means as a traction channel (as opposed to 'sales in general'). Cover: (1) the definition, (2) the structural conditions under which it fits, (3) the quick diagnostic a founder can run to tell if it's their channel, and (4) one concrete failure mode of assuming sales is your channel by default.
rubric: States the lead-gen → qualify → close definition precisely
Names interpersonal interaction / price / complexity as the fit signal
Gives the self-serve vs. human-in-the-loop diagnostic
Identifies 'B2B = sales' as familiarity bias or the ACV-too-low failure mode
Explanation is causal — explains why the human is load-bearing, not just lists features

concepts.title: Pipeline Math: The Funnel That Decides Whether Sales Works

pretest.prompt: You want to hit $100K/month in new MRR. Your average contract is $1,000/month ($12K ACV) and your close rate on qualified leads is 20%. Assume each AE can work 40 qualified-lead conversations per month. Before reading on — compute: (1) how many new deals per month you need, (2) how many qualified leads per month that requires, (3) how many AEs (minimum) that requires, and (4) roughly how big your top-of-funnel has to be if only 10% of raw leads qualify. What's the first number on this list you'd distrust most, and why?
pretest.modelAnswer: (1) $100K/mo new MRR ÷ $1K/mo per deal = 100 new deals/month. (2) At a 20% close rate, you need 100 / 0.20 = 500 qualified-lead conversations/month. (3) At 40 conversations per AE, that's 500 / 40 = 12.5 AEs — round up to 13. (4) If 10% of raw leads qualify, you need 500 / 0.10 = 5,000 raw leads/month feeding the top of the funnel. The number to distrust most is the 20% close rate — founders routinely project best-case close rates from small-sample founder-led deals that won't hold up when a newly-hired AE runs the same calls. A 20% close rate that's really a 7% close rate turns a 13-AE plan into a 37-AE plan, or the target from $100K/mo to $35K/mo. This is why founder-led sales with measured conversion rates has to come before any hiring plan — the inputs to the math are the whole game.

lesson.markdown:
## Why pipeline math is the whole decision

Sales is the one traction channel where the unit economics aren't soft. Every other number — how many AEs you can hire, how big a top-of-funnel you need, what you can spend to generate a lead, whether a VP of Sales hire makes sense — falls out of a few conversion rates and deal sizes multiplied through a funnel. If you don't do this arithmetic, you can't tell whether your sales channel is a machine that prints money or a bonfire burning cash on commissions.

The funnel has three stages the chapter cares about:

1. **Generate leads** — prospects enter the top of the funnel, usually from other traction channels (content, SEO, targeting blogs, outbound, events).
2. **Qualify leads** — decide which prospects are worth an AE's time; the rest are either nurtured or dropped.
3. **Close leads** — convert qualified prospects to paying customers through demos, negotiation, and commitment.

Each stage has a conversion rate. Multiply them and you get the end-to-end rate. Multiply *that* by your average deal size and you get revenue per raw lead — which, compared against your cost per raw lead, tells you whether the channel works.

## A worked pipeline

Suppose you're selling a $10K/year SaaS product and you want to hit $100K/month in new MRR (roughly $1.2M ARR annualized from new logos). Work backward:

- **Target:** $100K/mo new MRR ÷ ~$833/mo per deal = **120 new closed deals per month**.
- **Close rate on qualified opportunities:** say 25%. You need **120 / 0.25 = 480 qualified opportunities per month**.
- **Qualification rate from raw leads:** say 20% of raw leads qualify. You need **480 / 0.20 = 2,400 raw leads per month** feeding the top.
- **AE capacity:** say an AE can handle 60 qualified opportunities per month (ramping, demos, follow-ups, admin). You need **480 / 60 = 8 AEs**.
- **CAC check:** if each AE costs $150K/year loaded ($12.5K/mo), plus marketing costs to produce 2,400 leads/mo at, say, $50/lead ($120K/mo), your monthly sales + marketing spend is $12.5K × 8 + $120K = $220K/mo. You're producing $100K in new MRR — so CAC payback on a $10K ACV deal is ~2.2 months of MRR × 1 deal, but against 120 deals that's $1.2M annualized for $2.64M annual spend. Not viable unless retention is high and LTV justifies it. Either the close rate is low, the deal is too small, or the lead cost is too high.

This is the whole game. Four numbers — deal size, close rate, qualification rate, AE capacity — plus one cost number — CAC — tell you whether sales works.

## The three regimes by ACV

A useful crude frame: sales economics work very differently across order-of-magnitude jumps in deal size.

- **~$10K ACV deals.** Inside sales only (no travel), short cycles (weeks), high-volume AEs (60–100 opportunities/month), tight qualification, heavy automation. One AE can produce $300–500K/year in closed deals. Needs a big top-of-funnel to feed it.
- **~$100K ACV deals.** Mid-market / upper-mid-market. Longer cycles (2–6 months), multi-stakeholder buying, formal procurement. AEs carry 20–40 opportunities at a time. One AE produces $1–2M/year.
- **~$1M+ ACV deals.** Enterprise. Field sales, travel, 6–18 month cycles, executive sponsorship required, RFP processes, lawyers. AEs may close 3–8 deals per year. One AE produces $3–10M/year. Cost structure can include pre-sales engineers, solution architects, and an executive sponsor.

The mistake that sinks a lot of startups: projecting enterprise pricing with inside-sales velocity, or inside-sales pricing with enterprise cycle times. The shape of the funnel has to match the ACV band.

## Why founders must measure before hiring

The pipeline math is only useful if the input numbers are real. The single most common failure: founders assume a 20%+ close rate based on a handful of founder-led deals (where the founder's conviction and product knowledge closed the deal), hire AEs against that assumption, and discover the actual sustainable close rate for non-founder humans is 5–8%. The plan was a 10-AE org producing $2M/year; the reality is the same org producing $500K/year with $3M in burn.

This is why **founder-led sales must come before hiring anyone**. The next concept covers that principle directly — but the anchor is here: you can't build a sales channel you haven't measured, and you can't measure one you haven't personally run first.

## What to track from day one

If sales is a plausible channel for you, instrument from the first call:

1. **Raw leads** — how many prospects entered the funnel this week.
2. **Qualification rate** — what fraction became qualified opportunities.
3. **Close rate on qualified opportunities** — what fraction of qualified prospects became paying customers.
4. **Average deal size** and **sales cycle length** (days from first contact to close).
5. **Time per deal** — how many hours of selling time each closed deal consumed.

Those numbers are what let you compare sales to other traction channels on cost per acquired customer and predict whether scaling is viable. Without them you're not running a channel; you're running a hope.

workedExample.setup: Nadia sells TrialTrack, a $2K/month ($24K ACV) SaaS product for clinical research coordinators at mid-market hospitals. She's been running founder-led sales for six months with these measured numbers: 120 raw leads/month (from targeted blog sponsorships + cold email); 25% of raw leads become qualified opportunities; 20% of qualified opportunities close; average sales cycle 60 days; she personally spends about 5 hours per closed deal (from first demo to signed contract). She wants to model whether hiring two AEs can get her to $150K/month in new MRR within 12 months.
workedExample.walkthrough:
**Step 1 — Compute current baseline output.**

- Raw leads: 120/mo
- Qualified opportunities: 120 × 0.25 = 30/mo
- Closed deals: 30 × 0.20 = 6/mo
- New MRR from those deals: 6 × $2K = $12K/mo
- Founder time consumed: 6 deals × 5 hrs = 30 hrs/mo — sustainable as a side load

She's at $12K/mo new MRR from founder sales. To get to $150K/mo new MRR, she needs ~12.5× her current deal output — 75 deals/mo instead of 6.

**Step 2 — Work backward from the target.**

- Target closed deals/mo: $150K / $2K = 75
- At 20% close rate: qualified opportunities needed = 75 / 0.20 = 375/mo
- At 25% qualification rate: raw leads needed = 375 / 0.25 = 1,500/mo
- Current leads = 120/mo → she needs 12.5× her top-of-funnel

**Step 3 — AE capacity.**

At $24K ACV with a 60-day cycle, each AE can realistically carry 30–40 qualified opportunities at a time. Call it 35. So 375 qualified opps/mo / 35 = **10.7 AEs** — round up to 11. Not 2. Her mental model is off by ~5×.

**Step 4 — Sanity-check the close rate.**

This is the critical stress test. Nadia's 20% close rate was measured with her — the founder — closing deals. Hiring AEs typically produces a close rate 30–50% below founder-led rates for the first 6–12 months. If the sustainable AE close rate is 10% instead of 20%:

- Deals per qualified opp: 10%
- Qualified opps needed: 75 / 0.10 = **750/mo** (double the prior estimate)
- Raw leads needed: 750 / 0.25 = **3,000/mo** (25× current)
- AEs needed: 750 / 35 = **22 AEs**

At a 10% close rate the plan is 22 AEs, not 11. The sensitivity to the close-rate assumption is enormous — and she has no data on what the AE close rate actually is.

**Step 5 — What Nadia should actually do.**

Instead of committing to 11 AEs or 22 AEs, the disciplined move is:

1. **Hire one AE**, not two — the first hire is diagnostic, not capacity.
2. Ramp the AE on a copy of her founder-led playbook and measure their close rate on the next 30 qualified opportunities.
3. While the AE ramps, invest in lead-gen to push top of funnel from 120 to 400/mo — a 3× step that's testable without committing to the full 25× needed later.
4. After 90 days, recompute every number. If AE close rate is 15%+, scale AE count confidently. If it's 6–8%, either the product needs more productization of the sales motion (demos, collateral, objection-handling playbooks) or sales is going to be more expensive than the Bullseye comparison assumed.

She committed nothing she couldn't measure. The pipeline math didn't give her an answer — it gave her the cost of being wrong on each number, and told her which number to measure first.

contrast.explanation: The viral k-factor and the sales pipeline are the same pattern — multiply a chain of conversion rates to find out whether a channel compounds, sustains, or decays — applied to very different economics. Viral math asks whether the loop is self-sustaining (k > 1) or a useful amplifier (k > 0.5). Sales math asks whether CAC under the resulting funnel is below LTV, and whether a realistic AE headcount can produce the target revenue. In both cases the failure mode is the same: skipping the decomposition, working on whichever sub-step is most salient, and missing the one that's actually the bottleneck. In both cases the cure is the same: instrument each stage, measure real numbers, and target optimization at the weakest link.

flashcards.front: What are the three stages of the basic sales funnel?
flashcards.back: (1) Generate leads — prospects enter the top, usually via other traction channels. (2) Qualify leads — decide which are worth AE time. (3) Close leads — convert qualified opportunities to paying customers through demos, negotiation, and commitment.

flashcards.front: To hit $100K/mo new MRR on a $2K/mo product with a 25% close rate on qualified opportunities, you need ______ closed deals per month, which requires ______ qualified opportunities per month.
flashcards.back: 50 ; 200 — $100K / $2K = 50 deals; 50 / 0.25 = 200 qualified opps. The math is deliberately simple because it's the numbers going into it (deal size, close rate, qualification rate) that are hard.

flashcards.front: Why is the pipeline math only as good as the input numbers, and which input is most dangerous to assume?
flashcards.back: Because every downstream estimate (AEs needed, lead volume needed, CAC) is a multiplication through conversion rates — small errors compound. The most dangerous number is the close rate, because founders tend to project founder-led close rates (which include conviction, domain expertise, and personal credibility) onto hired AEs, who typically close at 30–50% lower rates during ramp.

flashcards.front: Name the three rough ACV regimes and one structural difference in the sales motion each requires.
flashcards.back: ~$10K ACV: inside sales, high-volume AEs (60–100 opps), short cycles. ~$100K ACV: mid-market, multi-stakeholder buying, 2–6 month cycles, 20–40 opps per AE. ~$1M+ ACV: field sales, RFPs, pre-sales engineers, 6–18 month cycles, a few deals per AE per year. The shape of the funnel has to match the ACV band; mismatching them is a classic failure.

flashcards.front: A founder is projecting a 25% close rate for a new sales team based on her own founder-led history. Which pipeline-math practice would flag this?
flashcards.back: Stress-testing input assumptions by recomputing the plan under a lower close rate (e.g., 10–12%, the common newly-hired-AE rate during ramp). If the plan goes from 2 AEs to 10 AEs when close rate halves, the founder is exposed to an assumption she hasn't yet measured — and the fix is to hire one diagnostic AE first, measure, then scale.

scenarios.setup: A founder sells a $6K/year product. He wants to hit $60K/month in new MRR ($60K × 12 = $720K/year in new ARR). He currently closes 30% of qualified opportunities. He can produce about 50 qualified opportunities per month through content + cold email today.
scenarios.question: Do the pipeline math: how many closed deals does he need per month, is his current lead volume enough, and what's the first lever he should pull?
scenarios.modelAnswer: New MRR target: $60K/mo. ACV is $6K/year = $500/mo per deal. Closed deals needed: $60K / $500 = 120 deals/month. At 30% close rate, qualified opportunities needed: 120 / 0.30 = 400/month. Current volume: 50/month. Gap: 8× more qualified opportunities needed. This is primarily a top-of-funnel problem, not a close-rate problem. The first lever is generating 8× more qualified opportunities — which at his current qualification rate means scaling raw leads by 8×. The channels most likely to produce that volume for a $500/mo product are probably content marketing at scale, SEO, and possibly paid acquisition — not more cold email (which tends not to scale 8× without degrading). He should also check: at $500/mo per deal, can AE time per deal economically support human closing? At say 3 hours per deal × 120 deals = 360 hours/mo = 2+ AEs. Total AE cost ~$25K/mo + lead costs; new MRR of $60K/mo. CAC payback looks tight but workable if retention is long. Pull lever 1: scale top of funnel; hold close rate constant until you see whether volume causes it to degrade.
rubric: Correctly computes 120 deals/mo and 400 qualified opps/mo
Identifies the 8× top-of-funnel gap as the bottleneck
Distinguishes cold-email limits from content/SEO scalability
Does a rough AE capacity + cost check
Names 'scale top of funnel' as the first lever, not 'improve close rate'

scenarios.setup: A team has $200K ARR from 10 customers (~$20K ACV). They want to model: scenario A — hire 4 AEs now and scale aggressively; scenario B — hire 1 AE as a diagnostic and delay others until measured close rate is in hand. The founder's own close rate has been 22%. Industry benchmarks for enterprise-SaaS inside sales suggest 7–10% close rate is typical for new AEs in their first year.
scenarios.question: Using pipeline math, contrast scenarios A and B in terms of forecast range and downside risk. Which is more disciplined and why?
scenarios.modelAnswer:
Scenario A (hire 4 AEs). Each AE can carry ~30 qualified opps/mo → 120 qualified opps/mo across the team. At 22% close rate (founder's rate): 26 deals × $1.67K/mo = ~$43K new MRR/mo. At 8% close rate (realistic new-hire rate): 10 deals × $1.67K/mo = ~$16K new MRR/mo. AE loaded cost ~$140K/yr × 4 = $560K/yr burn. If close rate is 22%: revenue covers cost with margin. If close rate is 8%: revenue is about a third of AE cost — burning $400K/year to discover the close rate assumption was wrong. Downside risk: high and hard to reverse (firing AEs is slow, culturally damaging, and forensically hard to disentangle from other variables).

Scenario B (hire 1 AE diagnostically). AE carries ~30 qualified opps/mo. At 22%: 6.6 deals × $1.67K = ~$11K/mo new MRR. At 8%: 2.4 deals × $1.67K = ~$4K/mo new MRR. Loaded cost ~$140K/yr. In 90 days you have real data on this AE's close rate with the playbook. If close rate clears 15%, hire 3 more with confidence; if it's below 10%, invest in productizing the sales motion (playbooks, collateral, objection handling, better qualification) before adding headcount. Downside risk: much smaller (single hire, reversible, produces the data needed for the real decision).

Scenario B is more disciplined because it buys the close-rate measurement before committing capacity. The founder's 22% is evidence the deal can be won, not evidence any AE can win it at the same rate. Pipeline math without real inputs is spreadsheet fiction; the purpose of scenario B is to replace the most critical assumption with data before the cost of being wrong scales 4×.
rubric: Computes pipeline output under both close-rate scenarios for A and B
Frames A's downside as large, irreversible, and diagnostically uninformative
Frames B's downside as small and diagnostic
Distinguishes founder-led close rate from measurable AE close rate
Recommends B and names the 90-day measurement as the decision trigger

scenarios.setup: A team sells a $500K ACV enterprise platform. They currently have 3 AEs who each close 6 deals/year with a 12-month average cycle. The CEO says 'we need to triple revenue next year — hire 6 more AEs.'
scenarios.question: What's wrong (or risky) with the linearity assumption in the CEO's math, and what would a sharper pipeline model ask instead?
scenarios.modelAnswer: The CEO is modeling AEs as if they're interchangeable and linearly additive. For $500K ACV enterprise sales with 12-month cycles, that's often wrong for several compounding reasons. (1) Ramp time: new enterprise AEs typically take 9–18 months to productive close rates; 6 new AEs hired today produce most of their value in year 2, not year 1. (2) Pipeline quality: each new AE needs qualified opportunities to work, and enterprise-quality pipeline is not cheap or fast to build — 9 AEs at 6 closes/year means 54 deals/year, which may require ~270+ qualified enterprise opportunities/year, which in turn may require hundreds of thousands of top-of-funnel touches, events, partnerships, or ABM spend that has its own lead time. (3) Deal concentration and support: at $500K ACV, each deal often requires a pre-sales engineer, a solution architect, sometimes an executive sponsor — scaling AEs without scaling that support creates a bottleneck that caps close rate. (4) Sales management: 3 AEs fit under a founder or first sales leader; 9 AEs needs a formal sales management layer. Sharper pipeline model asks: what's realistic close rate for new AEs in months 1–6 vs. 7–12 vs. 13+? What qualified-opportunity capacity does our current marketing / SDR org produce, and what does 3× output require? What's the pre-sales support ratio we need? What's the sales management structure? Answering those replaces 'hire 6 AEs' with a staged plan that might look like 2 AEs in Q1, 2 in Q2, 2 in Q3 with pipeline + pre-sales scaling in parallel — same total, very different risk profile.
rubric: Identifies AE linearity as a false assumption for enterprise
Names ramp time as a primary reason
Names pipeline quality / top-of-funnel capacity as a second bottleneck
Raises pre-sales / solution-architect support scaling
Raises sales-management layer
Prescribes a staged hiring plan with parallel scaling of supporting inputs

teachback.prompt: Teach pipeline math to a founder who is about to hire their first two AEs. Cover: (1) the three-stage funnel and what conversion rate belongs at each, (2) how to work backward from a revenue target to the required number of raw leads and AEs, (3) why the close-rate input is the most dangerous number, and (4) why the math's real purpose is to identify which assumption to measure before committing headcount.
rubric: Names the three stages and associated conversion rates (qualification rate, close rate)
Demonstrates the backward-computation with at least one concrete example
Flags close rate as the high-leverage input and founder-led rate vs. AE rate as the key asymmetry
Articulates that pipeline math's purpose is decision-making under uncertainty, not precise forecasting
Recommends a measure-before-scale practice (one diagnostic AE before a full team)

pretest.prompt: A technical founder is about to post a job ad for a VP of Sales before the company has closed a single paying customer. The pitch: 'I'm bad at sales; we need someone who's good at it to come in and build this.' What's wrong with that reasoning, and what would you recommend instead?
pretest.modelAnswer: The reasoning sounds humble but is actually a category error. A VP of Sales is hired to scale a sales motion that already exists — they are a force multiplier on a known playbook, not an inventor of one. If zero customers have been closed, there is no playbook yet: nobody knows what the pitch is, which objections come up, which prospect profile converts, what the close rate actually is, or what pricing holds up. A VP of Sales parachuted into that ambiguity either (a) invents a motion from scratch using generic best practices that may not fit the product, (b) burns six months trying to hire AEs before the machine they're supposed to run exists, or (c) quits when they realize there's no foundation to build on. The correct order is founder-led sales first: the founder personally closes the first 10–25 customers, listens to objections, iterates the pitch, measures conversion rates, and only *then* hires a sales leader whose job is to take that working motion and scale it.

lesson.markdown:
## The load-bearing principle

The single most common, most expensive sales mistake for early-stage founders: hiring a head of sales or a team of AEs before the founder has personally closed enough deals to know what works. This isn't a style point. It's structural — hiring sales before a repeatable motion exists almost always produces worse outcomes than founder-led sales with no hires, and it's reversible only at high cost.

## Why founders, specifically, close the first deals

First customers are different from customers-at-scale. Three reasons founder-led sales works during the first 10–25 deals and hired sales often doesn't:

1. **Domain credibility.** Founders of B2B products often have unusual depth in the domain — they're building the product because they lived the problem. That credibility is a huge fraction of why early prospects agree to a meeting, to a trial, to a purchase. An AE won't have it on day one.
2. **Product flexibility.** Early deals often close because the founder can say 'yes, we'll build that' or 'yes, we'll change that contract term.' An AE cannot make those commitments alone. The founder's authority to reshape the product in response to prospect feedback is itself a sales tool.
3. **Learning loop.** Every objection, every lost deal, every pricing question is information the founder needs for product, positioning, pricing, and eventually the playbook the sales team will run. Handing that learning to a hired AE means the founder never sees the signal.

Put together: founders are uniquely qualified to close first deals, and the first deals are uniquely valuable as learning input for everything else.

## What founder-led sales produces

The output of founder-led sales isn't just revenue. It's a concrete set of artifacts that make the next-stage hire successful:

- **Measured conversion rates** at each funnel stage (qualification, close, cycle time).
- **A written pitch deck or demo script** that has been iterated through dozens of actual calls.
- **A qualification checklist** — you know which prospect profiles convert and which waste time.
- **An objection library** — the top 10 objections and the best response to each, tested live.
- **Reference customers and case studies** — 5–10 named customers a hired AE can use for social proof.
- **A pricing model that holds up** — you've seen what people will and won't pay.

Only after those artifacts exist does hiring a sales leader make sense. Their job then is clear: scale this motion. Without the artifacts, the sales leader is being asked to invent the job and execute it simultaneously, and most fail.

## The 'I'm bad at sales' trap

Technical founders often say 'I'm bad at sales, so I need to hire someone who's good at it.' This sounds like self-awareness; it's usually avoidance. Two problems with it:

First, founder sales is not a skill competition with professional salespeople. Founders close early deals not because they're silver-tongued but because they know the problem, own the roadmap, and carry conviction. A competent founder with genuine domain knowledge outperforms a generic AE at the 0-to-25-customer stage almost every time.

Second, 'bad at sales' usually means 'uncomfortable with rejection' or 'doesn't like cold calls.' Both are legitimately unpleasant. But the discomfort is not a reason to hire — it's the reason founder-led sales is high-leverage: most founders won't do it, so the ones who do have a structural advantage. Outsourcing discomfort to a hire doesn't make the discomfort go away; it moves it into a compensation expense and buries the learning.

## When to hire the first sales person

A working heuristic: **hire your first sales person only after you've personally closed ~10 deals with the current product and pricing, and you have a repeatable motion you could write down in a doc.** Some founders hire slightly earlier (at 5 deals, if the motion is obviously working); some later (at 25, for longer-cycle enterprise products). The signal isn't a fixed number — it's 'I could describe exactly what I do to close, and I need more hours in the day, not a different person.'

The first hire in that world is typically an SDR/AE hybrid who works the founder's playbook, or an experienced AE who can run existing opportunities. A VP of Sales before that is premature; it's hiring a scaler before you have the thing to scale.

workedExample.setup: Kenji is a technical co-founder of AuditMap, a $36K/year compliance-mapping SaaS for SOC 2 auditors. They've built the MVP, have three pilot customers (two paid, one free), $2M in the bank, 12 months of runway. Kenji's instinct: post a VP of Sales job, hire quickly, let them build the sales team while he focuses on product. His co-founder, a former sales leader, is pushing back.
workedExample.walkthrough:
Apply the founder-led-sales principle.

**What's missing for a VP of Sales to succeed?** (1) Measured conversion rates — unknown, only 3 deals. (2) A written pitch — doesn't exist. (3) A qualification checklist — can't be built from 3 deals. (4) Objection library — hasn't been built because Kenji hasn't sat through enough rejection loops. (5) Reference customers — only 2 paid, not enough for diverse case studies. (6) Pricing that holds up — $36K was a guess; nobody has pressure-tested it. A VP of Sales hired today has nothing to scale.

**Downside of hiring now.** Loaded VP of Sales cost ~$300K/yr. At 12 months runway, that's 25% of remaining capital on one hire whose first 6 months will be 'figure out what we should sell, to whom, at what price' — work Kenji is better positioned to do. Likely outcome: 9 months in, Kenji has handed over his domain authority, the VP is pitching a product he doesn't fully grasp, pricing is shifting, AEs are complaining about the playbook. Burn is up, learning is down, and at month 12 they're arguing about whether to fire the VP.

**What Kenji should do for the next 6 months.**

1. **Personally close 10 more customers.** Target: go from 3 → 13. His co-founder can assist on pipeline but Kenji runs every first call and every close. Aim for 1–2 closes per month.
2. **Instrument the funnel.** Track raw leads, demo conversion rate, close rate, cycle time, time per deal. By deal 13, have honest numbers.
3. **Build the artifacts.** After each call, log objections, notes, what worked. By deal 13, have a pitch deck that's been iterated, a qualification checklist, an objection doc, and 5–8 reference-able customers.
4. **Test pricing properly.** Try $30K, $36K, $48K across deals. See where prospects push back. Lock a pricing model that converts.
5. **Consider first hire at deal 10–13.** First hire is an AE (or SDR + AE), not a VP. Their job: execute the documented playbook on 20 new opportunities per month, measured against Kenji's baseline close rate.
6. **VP of Sales later, if ever.** When AE #1 is producing at 70% of Kenji's close rate and the company needs 3+ AEs to hit revenue goals, then a head of sales makes sense — someone to manage the expanding team, not invent the playbook.

**What Kenji trades in.** He trades about 15–20 hours/week of product time for sales time over the next 6 months. That's costly. But the alternative — 25% of runway spent on a VP hired into ambiguity — is more costly. And the learning loop he gets from personal sales (what prospects ask, what breaks, what resonates) compounds directly into product decisions he'd otherwise guess at.

Notice: this isn't about whether Kenji is 'good at sales' as a personality trait. It's about whether the company knows enough yet to be worth scaling. The answer at 3 deals is obviously no. The answer at 13 deals, with instrumented data and artifacts, is a real yes-or-no that a hiring plan can hang on.

contrast.explanation: Founder-led sales is the sales-channel version of cheap testing: before committing a team and capital, the founder personally runs the channel at small scale to generate real data. In Bullseye's test step (Chapter 2), the principle is 'learn channel economics cheaply before scaling.' Here, the equivalent is 'learn sales economics by running sales yourself before hiring anyone.' Both are testing protocols — and both fail in the same way, for the same reason, when founders skip them: you can't scale what you haven't measured, and you can't measure what you haven't personally run.

flashcards.front: Why should founders personally close the first ~10 customers before hiring a sales team?
flashcards.back: Three reasons: (1) domain credibility closes early deals that AEs can't; (2) founders can flex product, pricing, and contracts in ways AEs can't; (3) the learning loop — objections, lost deals, pricing feedback — is information the founder needs for product, positioning, and the playbook the future team will run. Skipping this step means hiring a team with no playbook to execute.

flashcards.front: A technical founder with 2 customers says 'I'm bad at sales, so I'll hire a VP of Sales to build this for us.' Which failure mode does this fit?
flashcards.back: Hiring a scaler before there's anything to scale. A VP of Sales is hired to multiply a working motion — with 2 customers, there's no working motion yet. The VP will spend 6–9 months inventing the job (pitch, pricing, qualification) that the founder is better positioned to invent, while consuming a large share of runway. The 'I'm bad at sales' framing is often avoidance of founder discomfort rather than a real skill gap.

flashcards.front: Founders should hire their first sales person only after they've personally closed approximately ______ deals and have a ______ motion they could write down in a document.
flashcards.back: 10 (5–25 depending on ACV and cycle) ; repeatable — the signal isn't a fixed number but the founder's ability to describe exactly what closes a deal and the need for more hours, not a different person.

flashcards.front: List three artifacts founder-led sales should produce before the first sales hire.
flashcards.back: Any three of: measured conversion rates at each funnel stage; an iterated pitch deck or demo script; a qualification checklist; an objection library with tested responses; reference customers for social proof; a pricing model that has survived prospect pushback. These artifacts are what make a hired AE productive from month one rather than lost for a quarter.

flashcards.front: What's the Gong-style / modern SaaS principle on early sales leadership hiring?
flashcards.back: The first sales hire is typically an AE (or an SDR+AE pair) who executes the founder's playbook — not a VP of Sales. A VP/head-of-sales hire makes sense only after 2–3 AEs are producing against a known playbook and the company needs team management and go-to-market strategy, not motion invention. Premature VP hires fail on ambiguity, not competence.

scenarios.setup: A founder says 'We're going to skip founder-led sales because my co-founder hates selling and I'm too busy on product. We'll hire a great AE instead.' They have 4 customers, $15K ACV.
scenarios.question: What's the risk in this plan, and what's the minimum correction that preserves their product velocity?
scenarios.modelAnswer: The risk: with 4 customers and no instrumented funnel, there's no playbook for the AE to execute. A hired AE will spend the first 3–6 months figuring out the pitch, which prospect profile converts, what objections come up, what pricing holds — work the founders are better positioned to do because they built the product. The AE will also likely close at a lower rate than the founders would, and the founders won't have the learning loop that tells them why. Minimum correction: one of the co-founders commits to running sales as their primary activity for 90–180 days, with a goal of closing another 8–12 customers and producing the artifacts (conversion rates, pitch, qualification list, objection doc, references). The founder who hates selling can focus on product. After 12+ total customers with measured economics, hire the first AE to execute the documented playbook. This trade preserves product velocity for one founder while buying the sales learning that makes any future hire actually work. Not doing it means either hiring a VP into ambiguity (expensive) or hiring an AE who underperforms and can't be diagnosed (equally expensive in learning terms).
rubric: Identifies 'no playbook yet' as the core risk
Specifies which co-founder should run sales and why division of labor works
Gives a measurable threshold (~12 customers, artifacts produced) before first hire
Distinguishes an AE executing a playbook from an AE inventing one
Preserves product work but doesn't let both founders off sales entirely

scenarios.setup: A founder has personally closed 14 customers at $60K ACV over 9 months. He has measured close rates, a standing pitch deck, an objection doc, and 5 named references. He's now debating: hire one senior AE at $200K OTE, or hire a VP of Sales at $300K+ to build a team faster?
scenarios.question: Which hire is right, and what's the general principle distinguishing the two cases?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Hire the AE first. The principle: hire the first 1–2 sales individual contributors (AEs) to execute the founder's working playbook and validate that the motion is reproducible by non-founders. A VP of Sales is the next hire after you've confirmed — via AE #1's performance — that the motion does reproduce and you need someone to manage the scaling team. Hiring a VP at 14 customers skips the reproducibility test: if AE #1 closes at 50% of the founder's rate, the playbook needs more productization before it scales; the VP has nothing to fix until that's clear. The VP also brings team-building and management expertise, which only pays off when there's a team big enough to manage. One AE doesn't need management; one AE needs a playbook and a coach (the founder). So: AE #1 now, measure for 90–180 days. If AE #1 closes at 70%+ of founder rate, hire AE #2–3. When the team is 2–3 AEs and a clear ceiling appears on founder management capacity, hire the VP.
rubric: Recommends the AE over the VP
Articulates the reproducibility test as the missing validation step
Distinguishes IC scaling (AEs) from management scaling (VP)
Gives a concrete trigger for when the VP becomes right
Notes that AE #1 is also a diagnostic on whether the playbook transfers

teachback.prompt: Explain to a technical founder why they must personally close the first 10+ customers before any sales hire, even if they hate selling. Cover: (1) why founder-led sales closes deals AEs can't at the early stage, (2) the specific artifacts founder-led sales produces for the first hire, (3) why the 'I'm bad at sales, hire someone good' framing is a trap, and (4) when and who to hire first — AE vs. VP.
rubric: Names domain credibility, product flexibility, learning loop as the three reasons
Lists at least three concrete artifacts (pitch, conversion rates, qualification checklist, objection doc, references, pricing)
Addresses the 'I'm bad at sales' framing as avoidance or discomfort, not a real skill argument
Prescribes AE-first, VP-later, with a threshold (founder motion reproducible by AE #1)
Gives a realistic number for the founder-led-sales stage (~10, with ACV-dependent variance)

concepts.title: Discovery and Qualification: SPIN, PNAME, and A/B/C Bucketing

pretest.prompt: You have 60 inbound leads this month from a content marketing campaign. You're a founder who can realistically run 20 serious sales conversations per month. Before reading on — what are the questions you must answer about each lead to decide which 20 to invest in, and what happens to the other 40?
pretest.modelAnswer: For each lead, you need answers roughly along these lines: (a) do they have a real problem that our product solves, and how painful is it? (b) do they have the authority or access to authority to buy? (c) do they have the budget or is the budget plausibly available? (d) what's their timeline — weeks, quarters, or unknown? Leads that clear all four become 'A' opportunities and get most of your time. Leads that answer some but not all become 'B' — worth a second touch in 3–12 months. Leads that don't clear the bar go to 'C' — they stay in a marketing-nurture track (newsletters, educational content) until something changes, but they don't consume sales time. The purpose of qualification isn't to disqualify — it's to allocate the scarce resource (founder/AE time) against the leads most likely to close in the next quarter, while keeping the rest warm at near-zero marginal cost.

lesson.markdown:
## What discovery and qualification are for

After you generate leads, the next problem is that most of them aren't worth your time — at least not right now. Some are researchers who will never buy. Some have the right role but no budget. Some have budget but no pain. Some have everything but won't decide for a year. Discovery and qualification are the steps that sort those cases apart so you invest your selling hours in the 20–30% of leads where a close is actually possible in the current cycle.

Three frameworks make this systematic: **SPIN** (how to structure the discovery conversation), **PNAME** (what facts you need before investing more time), and **A/B/C bucketing** (how to allocate time across the qualified set).

## SPIN: the structure of a discovery call

Neil Rackham's SPIN framework, drawn from studying 35,000 sales calls, organizes discovery into four question types asked in sequence:

1. **Situation questions** — what's the prospect's current setup? Size, structure, tooling. Useful but easy to over-ask; too many and the prospect feels extracted-from. Keep to one or two per call.
2. **Problem questions** — what's broken, frustrating, or painful in the current setup? Are they happy with the existing solution?
3. **Implication questions** — what happens because of that problem? Lost productivity, employee turnover, customer churn, regulatory risk? Implication questions make the cost of the problem concrete and urgent in the prospect's own mind.
4. **Need-payoff questions** — how much better would things be if this problem were solved? What would implementing a fix enable?

The sequence matters. Situation establishes fit; problem surfaces pain; implication inflates the perceived cost of the pain; need-payoff sets up the prospect to articulate, in their own words, why your solution matters. A pitch delivered before this sequence lands on a prospect who hasn't yet convinced themselves the problem is worth solving. A pitch delivered after lands on a prospect who already wants to buy.

## PNAME: the qualification checklist

After discovery, you need to decide whether this prospect is worth a second and third conversation. Todd Vollmer's PNAME is a compact checklist:

- **P — Process.** How does this company buy solutions like yours? Do they have procurement? Is there an approved-vendor list? Is this a new category for them?
- **N — Need.** How badly do they need this? Is it urgent (solving this quarter) or nice-to-have (someday)?
- **A — Authority.** Who signs? Is my contact the decision-maker, an influencer, or a researcher with no power?
- **M — Money.** Is there budget? What does not solving the problem cost them?
- **E — Estimated timing.** What's the realistic close date? This quarter? Next year?

PNAME is close kin to older frameworks like BANT (Budget, Authority, Need, Timeline) and to more detailed ones like MEDDIC (Metrics, Economic buyer, Decision criteria, Decision process, Identify pain, Champion). They all answer the same underlying question: do we have a real buyer, with real money, on a real timeline? If any of the letters come back negative, the deal is at best a 'B' or 'C' — and working it as if it were an 'A' is how AE hours disappear.

## A/B/C: allocate time by close probability

Mark Suster's simple bucketing rule turns qualification into an allocation rule:

- **A deals** — realistic shot of closing in the next 3 months. Get 66–75% of sales time.
- **B deals** — forecast to close within 3–12 months. Get the remaining 25–33%. Building pipeline takes time.
- **C deals** — unlikely to close in 12 months. **Get zero sales time.** They belong to marketing — nurtured through newsletters, educational content, and periodic re-qualification until something in their situation changes.

The central discipline: **C deals stay out of the sales pipeline.** Sales time is the most expensive resource in the funnel; spending it on a prospect who won't close in the window they're being spent for is misallocation, and it compounds because every hour on a C deal is an hour not spent on an A or B.

## Why the frameworks interlock

SPIN tells you what to ask on the first call. PNAME tells you what facts to extract from those answers. A/B/C tells you what to do with the conclusions. Applied together, they turn a pile of 100 inbound leads into 15 A deals getting serious attention, 30 B deals getting periodic touches, and 55 C deals on a marketing-nurture list — with each allocation based on measurable signals, not vibes.

Founders often fail qualification in a characteristic way: they treat *every* lead as potentially closeable because the lead contacted them, so they spend full sales effort on prospects who were never in-market. The frameworks exist specifically to prevent that — to turn 'they seem interested' into 'they have Process, Need, Authority, Money, and a Timeline within the next quarter, and here's the specific sentence they said that confirmed each.'

workedExample.setup: Jin, a founder selling a $25K/year IT-asset-management platform, has 45 inbound leads this month from a sponsored newsletter. He has about 30 hours a week to spend on sales. He runs a first discovery call with each lead over two weeks. The leads are a mix — some IT directors at mid-market firms, some junior sysadmins, some curious consultants, some students doing research.
workedExample.walkthrough:
**Step 1 — SPIN on every discovery call.** Jin opens each 20-minute call with one situation question (company size, current tool) and one problem question (what's painful about the current setup). If the prospect engages on pain, he moves into two or three implication questions (what does the problem cost them — overtime, audit risk, licensing overspend?). If implications resonate, he asks one need-payoff question (what would it mean to solve this?). This sequence consistently produces a prospect who, by minute 15, has verbally described both the pain and what a solution is worth — without Jin having pitched anything yet.

**Step 2 — PNAME after each call.** Jin scores each lead on a simple rubric:

- *Process.* Does the company have a procurement process? Is IT-asset-management a known category to them?
- *Need.* Was the pain real and current, or aspirational?
- *Authority.* Is this person the decision-maker, or do they loop in a CIO?
- *Money.* Did the budget question land on a real answer, or a shrug?
- *Estimated timing.* When would they realistically buy?

**Step 3 — A/B/C bucket by the end of the week.** Of 45 leads:

- **12 A deals** — IT directors with current pain, budget in Q3, and authority to sign or direct influence over signing. These get follow-up demos, reference calls, and security-review docs. Jin invests the majority of his week here.
- **18 B deals** — have genuine need but fail on one or two PNAME dimensions: timing is next year, or budget requires a rebid cycle, or the contact is a sysadmin who needs to bring in a director. Jin schedules a 30-day touch, sends a case study, and keeps them warm with a light-touch monthly email.
- **15 C deals** — students doing research, consultants scoping for their own clients, curious readers with no authority, prospects whose company just bought a competitor. These go into the marketing nurture list. Zero sales hours.

**Step 4 — Behavior change.** Before using the frameworks, Jin would have given 30-minute calls + follow-up demos to all 45 leads — consuming 40+ hours on prospects who mostly wouldn't close. With A/B/C, he spends 20 hours on the 12 A's (plenty of time per deal) and 5 hours on periodic B touches; the 15 C's consume zero sales time. In weeks 3–6, this converts 5 A deals to closes, where the old approach might have closed 3 (because attention was diluted across 45 prospects instead of concentrated on 12).

The lesson isn't that qualification frameworks are clever. It's that sales time is the scarce resource, and without a disciplined allocation rule, it gets eaten by prospects who were never going to buy — at the expense of the ones who would have.

contrast.explanation: Pipeline math tells you *how many* qualified opportunities you need to hit a revenue target; qualification frameworks (SPIN / PNAME / A/B/C) tell you *which* leads earn that 'qualified opportunity' label. The two interlock: bad qualification produces inflated opportunity counts with low real close rates, which makes the pipeline math lie. If your funnel shows 400 'qualified opportunities' but half are really C's that nobody filtered out, your 25% close rate is actually a 12.5% close rate when measured against the true qualified set — and you'll hire twice as many AEs as you need. The math is only honest if the qualification is honest.

flashcards.front: What does SPIN stand for, and why is the order of the questions important?
flashcards.back: Situation, Problem, Implication, Need-payoff. Order matters because each step sets up the next: Situation establishes fit; Problem surfaces pain; Implication inflates the cost of the pain in the prospect's mind; Need-payoff has the prospect articulate the value of a solution. Pitching before this sequence lands on a prospect who hasn't yet convinced themselves the problem is worth buying a solution for.

flashcards.front: PNAME stands for Process, ______, Authority, Money, and ______ Timing — a qualification checklist used to decide whether a lead is worth further sales time.
flashcards.back: Need ; Estimated — closely related to older BANT (Budget, Authority, Need, Timeline) and more elaborate MEDDIC frameworks.

flashcards.front: How does the A/B/C bucketing rule allocate sales time?
flashcards.back: A deals (close probable in 3 months) get 66–75% of sales time. B deals (close probable 3–12 months) get the remainder. C deals (unlikely to close in 12 months) get zero sales time — they belong to marketing for nurture until something changes. The central discipline: C deals do not consume AE hours.

flashcards.front: Which SPIN question type is most likely to be over-used to the point of hurting the call?
flashcards.back: Situation questions. The research showed that too many situation questions per call reduces close probability — prospects feel extracted-from and like they're giving information without receiving value. Keep situation questions to one or two per call and move quickly to Problem and Implication.

flashcards.front: Why does qualification discipline directly affect the honesty of pipeline math?
flashcards.back: Because pipeline math is a chain of conversion rates applied to an opportunity count. If the opportunity count is inflated by C deals mislabeled as qualified, the measured close rate drops artificially, the forecasted revenue drops, and the AE capacity plan is miscalibrated. Accurate qualification is what makes the pipeline numbers reflect reality instead of optimism.

scenarios.setup: A founder reviews a call recording with her AE. The AE spent 45 of 50 minutes asking detailed questions about the prospect's org chart, headcount, tool stack, reporting structure, and current vendor pricing — then delivered a 5-minute pitch. The prospect was polite but didn't commit to a next step.
scenarios.question: Diagnose the call using SPIN. What went wrong and what should the AE do differently next time?
scenarios.modelAnswer: The AE over-indexed on Situation questions — the org chart, headcount, stack, reporting structure, and pricing are all Situation data. Rackham's research specifically identified this as the failure pattern: prospects feel extracted-from when too many Situation questions land without value being returned, and executive buyers (who are time-pressed) disengage. The AE never moved into Problem questions (what's painful?), Implication questions (what does the pain cost?), or Need-payoff (what would solving it be worth?). The pitch landed on a prospect whose pain and its cost had never been made explicit — so the pitch had no anchor. Next time: one or two Situation questions maximum to establish fit, then move to Problem immediately, spend the middle of the call on Implication (which does most of the persuasive work), end with Need-payoff to set up a pitch the prospect is primed for. The test: by minute 15, the prospect should be articulating pain and cost in their own words, not answering fact-finding questions about their org chart.
rubric: Identifies over-use of Situation questions as the core failure
Cites the research-backed reason (too many situation questions reduce close probability)
Notes the missing Problem and Implication steps
Prescribes the correct pacing (~1–2 situation, then Problem early, Implication as the middle, Need-payoff before pitching)
Explains that the pitch lacks anchor without Implication

scenarios.setup: A sales team has 200 'qualified opportunities' in their pipeline, a 10% measured close rate, and keeps missing quota. A review shows that 40% of the 'qualified' opportunities have no identified decision-maker, 25% have no confirmed budget, and 30% have timelines of 'someday / exploring.' Most of them would be C's under strict qualification.
scenarios.question: Using PNAME and A/B/C, diagnose what's happening and prescribe a fix. What will the real close rate look like after the fix?
scenarios.modelAnswer: The team is mislabeling Cs as qualified. PNAME failures: missing Authority (40% have no decision-maker), missing Money (25% no budget), missing Estimated timing (30% 'someday'). Many of these 200 'qualified' opportunities are C deals that shouldn't be consuming sales hours at all. The effect on pipeline math: real qualified set is much smaller. If we say roughly 40–50% of the 200 are actually qualified under strict PNAME, real qualified count is ~80–100. The 20 deals that are closing (10% of 200) are almost all coming from that real qualified set — so the true close rate is closer to 20/90 ≈ 22%, not 10%. Fix: re-qualify the full pipeline strictly, demote ~100 opportunities to B or C, and reallocate AE time to the real A's. Close rate on qualified opps will look higher (22%+ instead of 10%) because the denominator is honest. AEs will close more deals in absolute terms because their time is no longer absorbed by C's. Forecasting becomes more accurate because pipeline count means something. The short-term emotional cost: the pipeline 'shrinks by 50%,' which looks bad in a dashboard but is actually the pipeline becoming real instead of theatrical.
rubric: Identifies mislabeled Cs as the root cause
Names which PNAME letters are failing (Authority, Money, Timing)
Recomputes close rate against the honest qualified set
Prescribes strict re-qualification and demotion to B/C / marketing nurture
Addresses the optics problem — pipeline shrinks but becomes real
Connects the fix to downstream pipeline-math honesty

Cover: (1) the SPIN question sequence and why order matters, (2) the PNAME checklist and what failing any one letter implies, (3) A/B/C bucketing and the hard rule about C deals, and (4) how bad qualification corrupts the pipeline math downstream.
